message.content:
## Web Searcher: competitive landscape — names already taken in AI research space

Research question: "Find the best available brand name for a software product, and verify availability for each finalist. 

THE PRODUCT: an autonomous "deep research" AI agent/pipeline. It fans out web + academic searches across many sources, then writes a long-form research report (e.g. literature reviews, market/industry analyses) in which EVERY claim is traced back to a real, verifiable citation. Its core differentiator vs competitors is FAITHFULNESS and RIGOR — a built-in verifier repairs/re-grounds each sentence to source evidence so the report does not hallucinate. Think of it as "a research analyst that never makes up a fact." Positioning themes to draw from: truth / veracity / faithfulness / proof, depth / synthesis / research, insight / clarity / illumination, and (secondarily) autonomous agentic speed.

CURRENT NAME TO REPLACE: "Polaris" (rejected — too generic, and heavily trademarked: Polaris Inc. snowmobiles, Polaris missiles, many software products). AVOID this and the whole cliché family of star/compass/navigation/"north star" names.

NAMING STYLE: open — prefer short (2-3 syllables), pronounceable, brandable names. Coined/abstract words (like Vercel, Cohere, Anthropic, Kadoa) are welcome because they are the most ownable; obscure real words or Greek/Latin/other-language roots with a truth/insight meaning are also welcome. Avoid the crowded, already-taken names in the AI-research space — explicitly check and EXCLUDE names already used by: Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, Genspark, Storm/STORM (Stanford), Ai2/OpenScholar, Scholarcy, Semantic Scholar, Deep Research (OpenAI/Google/Perplexity feature names), Glean, Hebbia, Vespa, and any obvious near-duplicates.

DELIVERABLE: a RANKED shortlist of 8-12 candidate names. For EACH candidate provide:
  1. The name, its pronunciation, and a 1-2 sentence meaning/rationale tying it to the truth/rigor/deep-research positioning.
  2. GitHub availability: is the org/username taken? (check github.com/<name>).
  3. Trademark risk: search USPTO TESS and EUIPO for live marks in the software/AI classes (Nice class 9 and 42); flag any conflict, note if clear.
  4. Domain availability: is <name>.ai and/or <name>.com registered/parked/available? Note approximate acquisition feasibility.
  5. Social handle: is @<name> (or a close variant) free on X/Twitter?
  6. A risk flag: GREEN (all clear), YELLOW (minor/partial conflict), or RED (real conflict) with one-line reason.
Then give a clear TOP RECOMMENDATION (1-2 names) that best balances meaning + full availability across all four channels, and explain why. Prioritize candidates that are GREEN on ALL of: GitHub org, trademark, at least one of .ai/.com, and a social handle. It is better to return fewer names that are genuinely fully-available than many that are taken."

Your angle: **competitive landscape — names already taken in AI research space** — Maps the crowded naming space so we can exclude near-duplicates of Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Hebbia, Glean, etc., and spot which semantic territories (truth, scholar, cite) are already claimed.
Search query: `AI research assistant startups 2025 2026 automated literature review citation agent company names list`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the web search results, here's a list of AI research assistant startups and companies focused on automated literature review and citation agents in 2025-2026:

## AI Research Assistant Companies & Tools:

**ResearchRabbit** - Provides visual citation networks and mind maps, operating on a freemium model since its November 2025 relaunch.

**SciSpace** - Focuses on summarizing research papers with extended database connections to PubMed, Google Scholar, and ArXiv, automated literature reviews, podcast features, and AI writing tools.

**Consensus AI** - Offers semantic search with citation analysis and features a consensus meter that shows agreement levels across studies, accessing 200M+ articles.

**Litmaps** - Specializes in literature tracking and citation mapping with visual citation maps, organization tools like tags and color codes, automated paper monitoring, and collaborative sharing.

**Listen Labs** - Designed for teams scaling from single studies to continuous research programs.

**NotebookLM** - Performs source-grounded synthesis of uploaded documents with responses limited to the user-provided source base.

**Undermind** - An AI research assistant that searches scientific papers on your behalf and returns cited answers.

**Scite** - The strongest AI literature review tool for citation context analysis in 2026.

**Elicit** - Helps you search, summarize, extract data from, and chat with academic literature.

**Paperguide** - Offers citation context plus discovery, screening, structured extraction, and review writing with every citation grounded back to its source paper.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the best AI tools for literature review and research citations in 2026:

## Top-Rated Tools

The best AI tools for research in 2026 include Paperguide, Perplexity, Semantic Scholar, Elicit, Consensus, Scite, ChatGPT, Paperpal, and SciSpace.

### Specialized Leaders

- **Paperguide**: Paperguide is the #1 literature review AI tool in 2026 because the AI research platform ships the full Plan, Search, Screen, Extract, Generate workflow with citation grounding throughout.

- **Elicit**: Elicit is noted for screening and extracting comparative data points from paper sets.

- **Consensus**: Consensus AI is an AI-powered academic search engine that helps search and analyze more than 200 million peer-reviewed research papers.

- **Scite**: For citation context, Scite is unique.

## Key Workflow Insight

The best AI for literature review in 2026 is not one tool but a stack. Elicit handles structured extraction. Consensus answers evidence-stance questions. Scite shows citation context. Research Rabbit and Litmaps visualise citation networks. ChatGPT and Claude handle synthesis writing.

## Important Considerations

Literature reviews depend on verifiable citations, not just plausible-sounding academic prose. Researchers still need to verify every reference, but traceable workflows reduce the risk of citation hallucination and make review writing far more manageable.

content.text: The searches surfaced the competitive landscape well. Let me capture the most relevant results, ranking by relevance to the naming/branding question.

results.title: 9 Best AI Tools for Research in 2026 (Free & Paid)

results.title: 7 Best Literature Review AI Tools in 2026 (Free + Paid)
results.snippet: Adds more claimed brand names beyond the exclusion list: ResearchRabbit, Litmaps, Undermind, plus Paperguide's 'citation grounding' framing. Useful for spotting that 'research/paper/lit + noun' compound names and the 'cite/scite' semantic slot are already occupied.

results.title: 7 Best AI Research & Analysis Agents in 2026 — Agentic.ai
results.snippet: Focuses specifically on autonomous 'deep research' agents (the exact product category), likely surfacing agentic-branded competitors and confirming that 'deep research' and 'agent' descriptors are generic feature names rather than ownable brands.

results.title: 11 Best AI Tools for Scientific Literature Review in 2026
